input.prompt:
Search the web for academic papers related to this research topic: "An LLM That's Careful With Its Words" - specifically about chain-of-thought prompting with explicit "thinking tokens" between every sentence causing more careful text generation.

Key search terms to use:
1. "chain of thought prompting" LLM generation quality
2. "thinking tokens" language model careful generation
3. "pause tokens" LLM reasoning
4. "filler tokens" transformer computation
5. "implicit chain of thought" generation
6. "think before you speak" language model

For each paper found, provide: title, authors, year, arXiv ID (if available), and a brief description of relevance. I need at least 10-15 relevant papers. Focus on:
- Chain-of-thought prompting (Wei et al. 2022 is foundational)
- Thinking/pause/filler tokens in transformers
- The effect of extra computation on LLM output quality
- Careful/deliberate text generation
- Inner monologue or scratchpad approaches
- "Let's think step by step" and similar prompting techniques
- Papers about how transformers use intermediate tokens for computation

Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use."}, "authors": [{"authorId": "1725412182", "name": "Robert Vacareanu"}, {"authorId": "2299156997", "name": "Anurag Pratik"}, {"authorId": "3373316", "name": "Evangelia Spiliopoulou"}, {"authorId": "2326254458", "name": "Zheng Qi"}, {"authorId": "2286236297", "name": "Giovanni Paolini"}, {"authorId": "2100694309", "name": "Neha Ann John"}, {"authorId": "2299290259", "name": "Jie Ma"}, {"authorId": "2287927460", "name": "Yassine Benajiba"}, {"authorId": "2299152033", "name": "Miguel Ballesteros"}], "abstract": "Many of the recent capabilities demonstrated by Large Language Models (LLMs) arise primarily from their ability to exploit contextual information. In this paper, we explore ways to improve reasoning capabilities of LLMs through (1) exploration of different chains of thought and (2) validation of the individual steps of the reasoning process. We propose three general principles that a model should adhere to while reasoning: (i) Relevance, (ii) Mathematical Accuracy, and (iii) Logical Consistency. We apply these constraints to the reasoning steps generated by the LLM to improve the accuracy of the final generation. The constraints are applied in the form of verifiers: the model itself is asked to verify if the generated steps satisfy each constraint. To further steer the generations towards high-quality solutions, we use the perplexity of the reasoning steps as an additional verifier. We evaluate our method on 4 distinct types of reasoning tasks, spanning a total of 9 different datasets. Experiments show that our method is always better than vanilla generation, and, in 6 out of the 9 datasets, it is better than best-of N sampling which samples N reasoning chains and picks the lowest perplexity generation."}, {"paperId": "a42e3b6e023228fdbc447b0aa9a2d2e8b8a94f34", "externalIds": {"DOI": "10.1109/TNNLS.2025.3644134", "CorpusId": 284349244, "PubMed": "41468327"}, "title": "Large Language Models Are Multitask Chain-of-Thought Prompting Optimizers.", "year": 2025, "openAccessPdf": {"url": "", "status": null, "license": null, "disclaimer": "Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://api.unpaywall.org/v2/10.1109/TNNLS.2025.3644134?email=<INSERT_YOUR_EMAIL> or https://doi.org/10.1109/TNNLS.2025.3644134, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use."}, "authors": [{"authorId": "2283305538", "name": "Feihu Jin"}, {"authorId": "2283526261", "name": "Ying Tan"}], "abstract": "Large language models (LLMs) have achieved striking performance across a broad range of reasoning benchmarks, yet the quality of their outputs remains acutely sensitive to prompt design. A meticulously engineered prompt can coax an LLM into correctly answering even highly complex questions, prompting a surge of research into techniques that boost prompt efficacy. Manual chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting and automated prompt generation have emerged as leading strategies. However, CoT exemplars must be painstakingly tailored to each task, making the process labor-intensive, while prompts optimized for a single task often fail to generalize. We introduce a simple yet powerful alternative: by treating the LLM itself as a multitask optimizer, we enable iterative self-refinement of prompts through natural language task descriptions and few-shot in-context learning (ICL). Recognizing that individual prompts exhibit task-dependent sensitivity, we further ensemble the top-performing prompts at inference time. Empirical evaluation with several state-of-the-art LLMs shows that our method substantially surpasses prior baselines, delivering gains of up to 6.0% on mathematical reasoning tasks and 10.2% on commonsense reasoning benchmarks."}, {"paperId": "df8ce8028a21f72a89538228eccdd48b73a99d64", "externalIds": {"DBLP": "journals/corr/abs-2502-15727", "ArXiv": "2502.15727", "DOI": "10.48550/arXiv.2502.15727", "CorpusId": 276576131}, "title": "Retrieval Augmented Generation Based LLM Evaluation For Protocol State Machine Inference With Chain-of-Thought Reasoning", "year": 2025, "openAccessPdf": {"url": "", "status": null, "license": null, "disclaimer": "Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.15727, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use."}, "authors": [{"authorId": "2346984139", "name": "Youssef Maklad"}, {"authorId": "2346981956", "name": "Fares Wael"}, {"authorId": "2096169125", "name": "W. Elsersy"}, {"authorId": "2346983973", "name": "Ali Hamdi"}], "abstract": "This paper presents a novel approach to evaluate the efficiency of a RAG-based agentic Large Language Model (LLM) architecture for network packet seed generation and enrichment. Enhanced by chain-of-thought (COT) prompting techniques, the proposed approach focuses on the improvement of the seeds'structural quality in order to guide protocol fuzzing frameworks through a wide exploration of the protocol state space. Our method leverages RAG and text embeddings to dynamically reference to the Request For Comments (RFC) documents knowledge base for answering queries regarding the protocol's Finite State Machine (FSM), then iteratively reasons through the retrieved knowledge, for output refinement and proper seed placement. We then evaluate the response structure quality of the agent's output, based on metrics as BLEU, ROUGE, and Word Error Rate (WER) by comparing the generated packets against the ground-truth packets. Our experiments demonstrate significant improvements of up to 18.19%, 14.81%, and 23.45% in BLEU, ROUGE, and WER, respectively, over baseline models. These results confirm the potential of such approach, improving LLM-based protocol fuzzing frameworks for the identification of hidden vulnerabilities."}, {"paperId": "02a571f2c38ec25ba1a6ff3c6bf2297cd640bced", "externalIds": {"ArXiv": "2508.01977", "DBLP": "journals/corr/abs-2508-01977", "DOI": "10.48550/arXiv.2508.01977", "CorpusId": 280421634}, "title": "TIBSTC-CoT: A Multi-Domain Instruction Dataset for Chain-of-Thought Reasoning in Language Models", "year": 2025, "openAccessPdf": {"url": "", "status": null, "license": null, "disclaimer": "Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.01977, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use."}, "authors": [{"authorId": "2340191210", "name": "Fan Gao"}, {"authorId": "2275607695", "name": "Cheng Huang"}, {"authorId": "4577369", "name": "N. Tashi"}, {"authorId": "2351808095", "name": "Yutong Liu"}, {"authorId": "50141063", "name": "Xiangxiang Wang"}, {"authorId": "1395602706", "name": "Thupten Tsering"}, {"authorId": "2350513105", "name": "Ma-bao Ban"}, {"authorId": "2350514280", "name": "Renzeg Duojie"}, {"authorId": "2338177409", "name": "Gadeng Luosang"}, {"authorId": "2195041814", "name": "Rinchen Dongrub"}, {"authorId": "2338177725", "name": "Dorje Tashi"}, {"authorId": "2350493590", "name": "Xiao Feng"}, {"authorId": "2256769995", "name": "Hao Wang"}, {"authorId": "2241970724", "name": "Yongbin Yu"}], "abstract": "To address the severe data scarcity in Tibetan, a low-resource language spoken by over six million people, we introduce TIBSTC-CoT, the large-scale, multi-domain Tibetan dataset automatically constructed via chain-of-thought prompting with large language models (LLMs). TIBSTC-CoT establishes a scalable and reproducible framework for dataset creation in low-resource settings, covering diverse domains and reasoning patterns essential for language understanding and generation. Building on this dataset, we develop the Sunshine-thinking LLM family, a series of Tibetan-centric LLMs equipped with chain-of-thought capabilities. Trained entirely on TIBSTC-CoT, Sunshine-thinking has demonstrated strong reasoning and generation performance, comparable to state-of-the-art (SOTA) multilingual LLMs. Our work marks a significant step toward inclusive AI by enabling high-quality Tibetan language processing through both resource creation and model innovation. All data are available: https://github.com/Vicentvankor/sun-shine."}, {"paperId": "a303b1c9aaf5b52d9419473feff7d00e4154ec03", "externalIds": {"ACL": "2024.bea-1.23", "DBLP": "conf/bea/StahlBNW24", "ArXiv": "2404.15845", "DOI": "10.48550/arXiv.2404.15845", "CorpusId": 269362090}, "title": "Exploring LLM Prompting Strategies for Joint Essay Scoring and Feedback Generation", "year": 2024, "openAccessPdf": {"url": "", "status": null, "license": null, "disclaimer": "Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.15845, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use."}, "authors": [{"authorId": "2187454927", "name": "Maja Stahl"}, {"authorId": "2298268678", "name": "Leon Biermann"}, {"authorId": "2298273018", "name": "Andreas Nehring"}, {"authorId": "2253596550", "name": "Henning Wachsmuth"}], "abstract": "Individual feedback can help students improve their essay writing skills. However, the manual effort required to provide such feedback limits individualization in practice. Automatically-generated essay feedback may serve as an alternative to guide students at their own pace, convenience, and desired frequency. Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong performance in generating coherent and contextually relevant text. Yet, their ability to provide helpful essay feedback is unclear. This work explores several prompting strategies for LLM-based zero-shot and few-shot generation of essay feedback. Inspired by Chain-of-Thought prompting, we study how and to what extent automated essay scoring (AES) can benefit the quality of generated feedback. We evaluate both the AES performance that LLMs can achieve with prompting only and the helpfulness of the generated essay feedback. Our results suggest that tackling AES and feedback generation jointly improves AES performance. I found the paper you're looking for! Here are the search results:

## Key Information About the Wei et al. 2022 Paper

The paper "Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models" demonstrates how reasoning abilities emerge naturally in sufficiently large language models via a simple method called chain of thought prompting, where a few chain of thought demonstrations are provided as exemplars in prompting. Experiments on three large language models show that chain of thought prompting improves performance on a range of arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic reasoning tasks.

**Paper Details:**
- **ArXiv ID:** 2201.11903
- **ArXiv URL:** https://arxiv.org/abs/2201.11903
- **Authors:** Jason Wei, Xuezhi Wang, Dale Schuurmans, Maarten Bosma, Ed Chi, Quoc Le, and Denny Zhou
- **First Submitted:** January 28, 2022

**Key Finding:**
Prompting a 540B-parameter language model with just eight chain of thought exemplars achieves state of the art accuracy on the GSM8K benchmark of math word problems, surpassing even finetuned GPT-3 with a verifier.

This paper has become highly influential in the field and established chain-of-thought prompting as a fundamental technique for improving reasoning capabilities in large language models.

Here are the search results for your query on filler tokens, transformer computation, and thinking tokens in language models from arXiv:

## Key Findings

Transformers can use meaningless filler tokens (e.g., '……') in place of a chain of thought to solve two hard algorithmic tasks they could not solve when responding without intermediate tokens. This research comes from a prominent arXiv paper titled "Let's Think Dot by Dot: Hidden Computation in Transformer Language Models" by Pfau et al.

**Main Discoveries:**

Learning to use filler tokens is difficult and requires specific, dense supervision to converge. The researchers provide a theoretical characterization of the class of problems where filler tokens are useful in terms of the quantifier depth of a first-order formula. For problems satisfying this characterization, chain-of-thought tokens need not provide information about the intermediate computational steps involved in multi-token computations.

**Broader Context on "Thinking Tokens":**

These tokens have gone by many names, such as pause tokens, memory tokens, filler tokens, and thinking tokens. These works mainly focus on noncontentful contemplation tokens, whose main advantage is their ability to be decoded in parallel, providing the model with a greater computational width without the need to autoregressively decode. They have been shown to increase the theoretical computational ability of Transformer LLMs, but cannot simply be naively applied to induce reasoning gains.

**Implications:**

The fact that intermediate tokens can act as filler tokens raises concerns about large language models engaging in unauditable, hidden computations that are increasingly detached from the observed chain-of-thought tokens.

Links: [{"title":"Think before you speak: Training Language Models With Pause Tokens","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2310.02226v2"},{"title":"[2310.02226] Think before you speak: Training Language Models With Pause Tokens","url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.02226"},{"title":"Pause Tokens Strictly Increase the Expressivity of Constant-Depth Transformers","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2505.21024v1"},{"title":"Think before you speak: Training Language Models With Pause Tokens | OpenReview","url":"https://openreview.net/forum?id=ph04CRkPdC"},{"title":"[2310.02226] Think before you speak: Training Language Models With Pause Tokens","url":"https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2310.02226"},{"title":"Learning to Insert [PAUSE] Tokens for Better Reasoning","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2506.03616v1"},{"title":"Token Assorted: Mixing Latent and Text Tokens for Improved Language Model Reasoning","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2502.03275v2"},{"title":"Reasoning Beyond Language: A Comprehensive Survey on Latent Chain-of-Thought Reasoning","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2505.16782v1"},{"title":"Enhancing Latent Computation in Transformers with Latent Tokens","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2505.12629"},{"title":"Training Large Language Models to Reason in a Continuous Latent Space","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2412.06769v3"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about pause tokens in LLM reasoning:

## Core Concept

Pause tokens are learnable tokens appended to the input prefix, with the model's outputs extracted only after the last pause token is seen, thereby allowing the model to process extra computation before committing to an answer.

## How They Work

Language models normally generate the (K+1)th token as an outcome of manipulating K hidden vectors per layer, but with pause tokens, the model can instead manipulate K+10 hidden vectors (for example) before outputting the (K+1)th token. This introduces new computational pathways between the prefix token and the output token of interest.

## Empirical Results

Inference-time delays show gains when the model is both pre-trained and finetuned with delays, with a 1B model witnessing gains on 8 of 9 tasks, including 18% EM score improvement on SQuAD and 8% on CommonSenseQA.

## Theoretical Insights

Recent work has provided theoretical foundations: With bounded-precision activations, Transformers without pause tokens compute only a strict subset of AC⁰ functions, while adding pause tokens allows them to express the entire class; for logarithmic-precision Transformers, pause tokens achieve expressivity equivalent to TC⁰.

## Distinct from Chain-of-Thought

Pause tokens are positioned as a distinct mechanism complementary to chain-of-thought prompting, acting as dedicated "workspace" positions that simplify how the Transformer routes and aggregates information across the sequence.

I found several highly relevant papers on this topic. Here are the key results:

## Main Papers Found:

**1. "Think Before You Speak: Cultivating Communication Skills of Large Language Models via Inner Monologue"** (arXiv:2311.07445)

This paper empowers LLMs with communication skills through inner monologues, inspired by linguistics and cognitive science. The paper notes that enabling communication skills in black-box LLMs remains a key challenge because they do not have the same utterance formation mode as real people: think before speaking. To implement inner monologue, the approach makes an LLM play two roles simultaneously: a thinking role that makes internal decisions about communication skills through inner monologue, and a speaking role that generates responses based on the thinking content and history utterances. The authors construct a benchmark named Cskills to evaluate various communication skills.

**2. "Inner Monologue: Embodied Reasoning through Planning with Language Models"** (arXiv:2207.05608)

This paper proposes that by leveraging environment feedback, LLMs are able to form an inner monologue that allows them to more richly process and plan in robotic control scenarios.

**3. "Quiet-STaR: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Think Before Speaking"** (arXiv:2403.09629)

This paper presents Quiet-STaR, a method in which LMs learn to generate rationales at each token to explain future text, improving their predictions. Results show zero-shot improvements on GSM8K and CommonsenseQA benchmarks.

**4. "Learning to Reason and Memorize with Self-Notes"** (arXiv:2305.00833)

This method allows the model to deviate from the input context at any time to explicitly think and write down its thoughts, enabling the model to perform reasoning on the fly as it reads the context.

Based on the search results, I found the paper you're looking for. Large language models are zero-shot reasoners by simply adding "Let's think step by step" before each answer, with experimental results demonstrating that this Zero-shot-CoT approach significantly outperforms zero-shot LLM performances on diverse benchmark reasoning tasks including arithmetics, symbolic reasoning, and other logic tasks.

The paper is authored by Takeshi Kojima and four other authors and was published on arXiv with the identifier 2205.11916.

This method differs from the original chain of thought prompting as it does not require step-by-step few-shot examples, and differs from most prior template prompting as it is inherently task-agnostic and elicits multi-hop reasoning across a wide range of tasks with a single template.

I found comprehensive information about the Quiet-STaR paper. Here's what the search reveals:

## Overview

Quiet-STaR is a generalization of STaR in which LMs learn to generate rationales at each token to explain future text, improving their predictions.

## Key Technical Approach

To resolve key challenges, the researchers propose a tokenwise parallel sampling algorithm, using learnable tokens indicating a thought's start and end, and an extended teacher-forcing technique. This directly addresses your mention of "thinking tokens every position."

## Main Innovation

The paper builds upon the Self-Taught Reasoner (STaR) framework. Instead of the LM learning to reason on particular tasks like mathematical QA, the researchers train an LM to generate reasoning that helps it infer future text from a large internet text corpus, allowing the LM to learn from the diverse tasks present in language.

## Results

After continued pretraining of an LM on a corpus of internet text with Quiet-STaR, the researchers find zero-shot improvements on GSM8K (5.9%→10.9%) and CommonsenseQA (36.3%→47.2%) and observe a perplexity improvement of difficult tokens in natural text. Crucially, these improvements require no fine-tuning on these tasks.

The paper was published on March 14, 2024, with arXiv ID 2403.09629, authored by Eric Zelikman, Georges Harik, and others including Noah D. Goodman.

Based on the web search results, here's what I found about implicit chain of thought reasoning in language models:

## Key Research on Implicit Chain of Thought

Implicit chain of thought reasoning is a concept for transformer-based language models where reasoning is performed "vertically" among the transformer hidden states, instead of being performed "horizontally" in the form of generating intermediate tokens. This concept potentially enables the model to break away from the human-like reasoning process and develop its own internal reasoning process.

## Notable Approaches

**Stepwise Internalization**: One approach called Stepwise Internalization achieves implicit chain-of-thought reasoning in language models by gradually removing intermediate CoT tokens and finetuning the model to enable the internalization of reasoning steps incrementally. This approach achieves high accuracy on up to 9 × 9 multiplication using GPT-2 Small and outperforms GPT-4 on GSM8K while not outputting any intermediate reasoning steps.

**Knowledge Distillation Approach**: A three-step approach proposes to distill the knowledge of a teacher trained for horizontal reasoning into a student and an emulator trained for vertical reasoning.

## Recent Survey

Recent studies have shifted attention from explicit chain-of-thought prompting toward implicit reasoning, where reasoning occurs silently via latent structures without emitting intermediate textual steps, bringing advantages such as lower generation cost, faster inference, and better alignment with internal computation.

These papers present various techniques for enabling language models to perform sophisticated reasoning internally rather than generating explicit step-by-step explanations.

I found detailed information about STaR (Self-Taught Reasoner) by Eric Zelikman and colleagues. Here's a summary of the research:

## Overview

STaR is a technique by Eric Zelikman, Yuhuai Wu, Jesse Mu, and Noah D. Goodman from Stanford University that improves language model performance on complex reasoning tasks like mathematics or commonsense question-answering through generating step-by-step "chain-of-thought" rationales.

## The Problem

Inducing language model rationale generation currently requires either constructing massive rationale datasets or sacrificing accuracy by using only few-shot inference.

## The Solution

STaR proposes a technique to iteratively leverage a small number of rationale examples and a large dataset without rationales, to bootstrap the ability to perform successively more complex reasoning.

The method repeats the following process: in each iteration, first construct a finetuning dataset by attempting to solve the dataset using the current model's rationale generation ability; then, augment this dataset using rationalization, justifying ground-truth answers to problems the model failed to solve; finally, finetune the large language model on the combined dataset.

## Results

Applying STaR on arithmetic, math word problems, and commonsense reasoning, it effectively translates a small number of few-shot prompts into a large rationale dataset, yielding dramatic performance improvements.

The paper was published on arXiv as entry 2203.14465 in March 2022.

I found the paper you were looking for! Here are the key details:

**Paper Title:** "Show Your Work: Scratchpads for Intermediate Computation with Language Models"

**Author:** Maxwell Nye and colleagues

**Publication Date:** November 30, 2021

**ArXiv ID:** 2112.00114

**Main Contribution:** The paper demonstrates that language models can perform complex multi-step computations when asked to perform operations "step by step" by emitting intermediate computation steps into a "scratchpad".

**Key Results:** On a series of increasingly complex tasks ranging from long addition to the execution of arbitrary programs, scratchpads dramatically improve the ability of language models to perform multi-step computations.

The paper addresses a fundamental limitation where language models struggle with tasks that require unbounded multi-step computation, such as adding integers or executing programs.

Based on the web search results, I found the paper you're looking for:

## "Learning to Reason and Memorize with Self-Notes" by Jack Lanchantin et al.

The paper proposes a method allowing models to take Self-Notes. Unlike recent chain-of-thought or scratchpad approaches, the model can deviate from the input context at any time to explicitly think and write down its thoughts. This allows the model to perform reasoning on the fly as it reads the context and even integrate previous reasoning steps, thus enhancing its memory with useful information and enabling multi-step reasoning.

The model can interleave generated tokens with the input context and question. Such Self-Notes can act as both explicit intermediate reasoning steps and working memory for state-tracking.

Experiments across a wide variety of tasks demonstrate that the method can outperform chain-of-thought and scratchpad methods by taking Self-Notes that interleave the input text.

**Paper Details:**
- **Authors:** Jack Lanchantin, Shubham Toshniwal, Jason Weston, Arthur Szlam, and Sainbayar Sukhbaatar
- **arXiv ID:** 2305.00833
- **Published:** May 2023 (Thirty-seventh Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems, 2023)

I've found several relevant research papers on arXiv related to your search query about transformer intermediate computation depth, adaptive computation, and extra tokens for reasoning. Here are the key findings:

## Main Research Areas

**1. Inner Thinking Transformer (ITT):** This approach enhances token-level reasoning by dynamically allocating additional thinking steps to key tokens using an Adaptive Token Routing architecture and Residual Thinking Connection mechanism.

**2. Depth-Recurrent Transformers:** The Huginn-3.5B model exemplifies a depth-recurrent Transformer that reuses layers at inference to increase computational depth per token, though token rank trajectory analysis provides little evidence for latent chain-of-thought reasoning.

**3. Adaptive Computation Mechanisms:** Research examines techniques to expand a model's effective depth at inference time, such as segment-level recurrence and Adaptive Computation Time (ACT).

**4. CoTFormer:** This work proposes a training method that allows adjusting the per-token depth distribution at inference, controlling computation costs while trading compute for accuracy.

**5. Pause Tokens:** Pause tokens can act as dedicated "workspace" positions that simplify how the Transformer routes and aggregates information, and having separate tokens for intermediate computations can reduce the complexity of attention patterns.

**6. Mixture-of-Recursions (MoR):** Recent findings show that looping enhances model reasoning capabilities, providing a foundation for bridging adaptive compute and latent reasoning.

**7. Critical Analysis:** Research found that trace lengths can look indicative of problem adaptive computation on in-distribution problems, but this correlation breaks down out-of-distribution, with models producing extremely long derivation traces even on trivial problems.

I've found several relevant research papers and resources on latent reasoning and latent chain-of-thought computation in transformers. Here are the key findings:

## Major Recent Papers (2024-2025):

1. **"A Survey on Latent Reasoning"** (July 2025, arXiv:2507.06203)
   - Latent reasoning performs multi-step inference entirely in the model's continuous hidden state, eliminating token-level supervision
   - Coconut loops the LLM's last hidden state (the "continuous thought") directly back into the model as input for the next step

2. **"Training Large Language Models to Reason in a Continuous Latent Space"** (Coconut, December 2024, arXiv:2412.06769)
   - Coconut utilizes the last hidden state of the LLM as a representation of the reasoning state, termed "continuous thought," and feeds it back to the model as the next input embedding directly in the continuous space
   - Continuous thoughts can encode multiple alternative next steps, allowing the model to perform a breadth-first search (BFS) rather than committing prematurely to a single deterministic path

3. **"Latent Chain-of-Thought? Decoding the Depth-Recurrent Transformer"** (July 2025, arXiv:2507.02199)
   - Recurrent methods pass intermediate continuous hidden states across reused layers to simulate multi-step reasoning without emitting language
   - Their findings reveal limited evidence of interpretable latent CoT by tracking rank trajectories of final and intermediate result tokens

4. **"A Formal Comparison Between Chain of Thought and Latent Thought"** (January 2026, arXiv:2509.25239)
   - Latent thought reasons directly in the hidden state space rather than in the discrete token space

5. **"Reasoning Beyond Language: A Comprehensive Survey on Latent Chain-of-Thought Reasoning"** (May 2025, arXiv:2505.16782)
   - Discusses alternative architectures and interpretability challenges in latent reasoning

6. **"Latent Reasoning in LLMs as a Vocabulary-Space Superposition"** (October 2025, arXiv:2510.15522)
   - Latent reasoning retains the last hidden state from the latent layer, bypassing token sampling and directly feeding it back as the next-step representation, which carries richer information than a single token

These papers cover the computational methods, architectural implementations, and theoretical analysis of how transformers can perform reasoning in their hidden state space rather than through explicit textual reasoning chains.

I found the research paper! Here's what the web search revealed:

## Tree of Thoughts: Deliberate Problem Solving with Large Language Models

The paper is authored by Shunyu Yao, Dian Yu, Jeffrey Zhao, Izhak Shafran, Thomas L. Griffiths, Yuan Cao, and Karthik Narasimhan, published in 2023 with arXiv ID 2305.10601.

### Overview

Language models are confined to token-level, left-to-right decision-making processes during inference, causing them to fall short in tasks that require exploration, strategic lookahead, or where initial decisions play a pivotal role.

### The Framework

Tree of Thoughts (ToT) is a new framework for language model inference that generalizes over the popular Chain of Thought approach to prompting language models, and enables exploration over coherent units of text (thoughts) that serve as intermediate steps toward problem solving.

ToT allows LMs to perform deliberate decision making by considering multiple different reasoning paths and self-evaluating choices to decide the next course of action, as well as looking ahead or backtracking when necessary to make global choices.

### Key Results

The experiments showed that ToT significantly enhanced language models' problem-solving abilities on three novel tasks: Game of 24, Creative Writing, and Mini Crosswords. In Game of 24, while GPT-4 with chain-of-thought prompting only solved 4% of tasks, the method achieved a success rate of 74%.

I found the paper! Here's a summary of the search results:

## "Let's Think Dot by Dot: Hidden Computation in Transformer Language Models"

The paper is by Jacob Pfau and 2 other authors (William Merrill and Samuel R. Bowman), published on arXiv on April 24, 2024 (paper 2404.15758).

### Main Findings

The research shows that transformers can use meaningless filler tokens (e.g., '......') in place of a chain of thought to solve two hard algorithmic tasks they could not solve when responding without intermediate tokens.

However, learning to use filler tokens is difficult and requires specific, dense supervision to converge.

### Theoretical Contributions

The authors provide a theoretical characterization of the class of problems where filler tokens are useful in terms of the quantifier depth of a first-order formula.

### Key Implications

The results show that additional tokens can provide computational benefits independent of token choice, and raise concerns about large language models engaging in unauditable, hidden computations that are increasingly detached from the observed chain-of-thought tokens.

The paper's code is available on GitHub at the repository mentioned in the search results.

I found the paper! Here's what the search results reveal:

## Paper Summary

The paper provides the first formal separation result, proving that adding pause tokens to constant-depth, logarithmic-width Transformers strictly increases their computational expressivity.

**Key Findings:**

- Pause tokens are simple filler symbols such as "...", and they consistently improve Transformer performance on both language and mathematical tasks, yet their theoretical effect remained unexplained.

- With bounded-precision activations, Transformers without pause tokens compute only a strict subset of AC⁰ functions, while adding a polynomial number of pause tokens allows them to express the entire class.

- For logarithmic-precision Transformers, the paper shows that adding pause tokens achieves expressivity equivalent to TC⁰, matching known upper bounds.

- Empirically, the paper demonstrates that two-layer causally masked Transformers can learn parity when supplied with pause tokens, a function that they appear unable to learn without them.

**Paper Details:**
- **Authors:** Charles London and Varun Kanade
- **arXiv ID:** 2505.21024
- **Published:** May 27, 2025
- **Acceptance:** The paper has been accepted for the 39th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2025), 2-7 December 2025, San Diego, CA, USA.

I found comprehensive information about the paper. Here's what I found:

## Overview

Coconut (Chain of Continuous Thought) is a new paradigm that utilizes the last hidden state of the LLM as a representation of the reasoning state, termed "continuous thought." Instead of decoding this state into words, it feeds the state back to the model as the next input embedding directly in the continuous space.

## Key Motivation

The language space may not always be optimal for reasoning. Most word tokens primarily ensure textual coherence and are not essential for reasoning, while some critical tokens require complex planning and pose challenges to LLMs.

## Core Innovation

The continuous thought can encode multiple alternative next reasoning steps, allowing the model to perform a breadth-first search (BFS) to solve the problem, rather than prematurely committing to a single deterministic path like CoT.

## Performance Results

Coconut outperforms CoT in certain logical reasoning tasks that require substantial backtracking during planning, with fewer thinking tokens during inference. Additionally, Coconut generates significantly fewer tokens during inference compared to CoT while often achieving comparable or better accuracy.

## Authors

The paper was authored by Hao, Shibo, Sukhbaatar, Sainbayar, Su, DiJia, Li, Xian, Hu, Zhiting, Weston, Jason, and Tian, Yuandong.

Based on the search results, I found the paper "Learning to Insert [PAUSE] Tokens for Better Reasoning" from arXiv 2506.03616. Here's what the research is about:

## Overview

The paper introduces Dynamic Inserting Tokens Training (DIT), a novel approach in which inserting dummy tokens before reasoning steps can enhance effectiveness.

## Core Method

The method identifies positions within sequences where model confidence is lowest according to token log-likelihood, and strategically inserts [PAUSE] tokens on these positions to bolster the model's predictive capabilities for subsequent tokens.

## Key Differences from Prior Work

Unlike previous studies that focused on the pre-training phase, this research demonstrates that significant performance gains can be achieved by strategically inserting [PAUSE] tokens during the fine-tuning phase, identifying optimal positions during fine-tuning and enhancing performance even in models not pre-trained with them.

## Experimental Results

The method shows performance improvements with gains of up to 4.7%p on GSM8K, 3.23%p on AQUA-RAT in accuracy, and a 3.4%p increase in pass@1 on MBPP.

## How It Works

By incorporating [PAUSE] tokens, the model can focus more on challenging tokens with higher loss, resulting in a higher training signal for harder tokens, improving training and inference.

The paper was published on June 4, 2025, and demonstrates effectiveness across different model sizes from 2.7B to 8B parameters.

Great! I found the paper you're looking for. Here's what I found about the "Compressed Chain of Thought" research:

## Overview

The paper proposes Compressed Chain-of-Thought (CCoT), a framework to generate contentful and continuous contemplation tokens of variable sequence length, with the generated contemplation tokens being compressed representations of explicit reasoning chains that can be applied to off-the-shelf decoder language models.

## Problem It Addresses

Chain-of-thought (CoT) decoding enables language models to improve reasoning performance at the cost of high generation latency in decoding. In contrast, Chain of Thought (CoT) prompting reasons via discrete language tokens, leading to long sequences that incur significant generation costs, while Compressed Chain of Thought (CCOT) elicits reasoning with a short sequence of continuous embeddings, allowing for much greater throughput.

## Key Innovation

The paper introduces the term "contemplation tokens," which refers to special tokens used during inference to allow for extra computation. Under CCOT, these tokens are a compressed representation of a full reasoning chain, equivalent to a size k subset of the hidden states produced by the language model.

## Results

CCoT enables additional reasoning over dense contentful representations to achieve corresponding improvements in accuracy.

**Paper Details:** arXiv:2412.13171, authored by Jeffrey Cheng and Benjamin Van Durme, published December 17, 2024.

Based on the search results, I found comprehensive information about Adaptive Computation Time (ACT):

## Overview

Adaptive Computation Time (ACT) is an algorithm that allows recurrent neural networks to learn how many computational steps to take between receiving an input and emitting an output. ACT requires minimal changes to the network architecture, is deterministic and differentiable, and does not add any noise to the parameter gradients.

## Key Concepts

ACT is a mechanism for dynamically modulating the number of computational steps needed to process each input symbol (called the "ponder time") in standard recurrent neural networks based on a scalar halting probability predicted by the model at each step.

## Mechanisms and Design

Adaptive Computation Time automatically learns to scale the required computation time via a scalar halting probability, which modulates the number of computational steps, called the "ponder time", needed for each input.

## Performance and Applications

Experimental results are provided for four synthetic problems: determining the parity of binary vectors, applying binary logic operations, adding integers, and sorting real numbers, and overall, performance is dramatically improved by the use of ACT, which successfully adapts the number of computational steps to the requirements of the problem.

## Extension to Transformers

Inspired by the interpretation of Universal Transformers as applying self-attentive RNNs in parallel to all positions in the sequence, a dynamic ACT halting mechanism can be added to each position (i.e., to each per-symbol self-attentive RNN), and once the per-symbol recurrent block halts, its state is simply copied to the next step until all blocks halt, or a maximum number of steps is reached.

I found the paper! Here are the search results for "Think before you speak: Training Language Models With Pause Tokens" (arXiv 2310.02226):

## Paper Overview

This paper was authored by Sachin Goyal, Ziwei Ji, Ankit Singh Rawat, Aditya Krishna Menon, Sanjiv Kumar, and Vaishnavh Nagarajan, and was submitted on October 3, 2023.

## Core Concept

The paper proposes allowing language models to manipulate additional hidden vectors before generating the next token, rather than producing tokens in immediate succession. The approach uses a learnable "pause" token appended to the input prefix, and delays extracting the model's outputs until the last pause token is seen, allowing the model to process extra computation before committing to an answer.

## Evaluation

The pause-training method was empirically evaluated on decoder-only models of 1B and 130M parameters with causal pretraining on C4, and on downstream tasks covering reasoning, question-answering, general understanding and fact recall.

The paper was published at ICLR 2024 and is available at https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.02226.

Based on the search results, here's information about the paper arXiv 2311.01460:

## Paper Overview

The paper "Implicit Chain of Thought Reasoning via Knowledge Distillation" is authored by Yuntian Deng, Kiran Prasad, Roland Fernandez, Paul Smolensky, Vishrav Chaudhary, and Stuart Shieber, and was published on November 2, 2023.

## Key Concept

Instead of explicitly producing chain of thought reasoning steps, the paper explores using the language model's internal hidden states to perform implicit reasoning. The implicit reasoning steps are distilled from a teacher model trained on explicit chain-of-thought reasoning, and instead of doing reasoning "horizontally" by producing intermediate words one-by-one, the reasoning happens "vertically" among the hidden states in different layers.

## Main Contribution

This work enables solving tasks previously not solvable without explicit chain-of-thought, at a speed comparable to no chain-of-thought. The key motivation is that although people use natural language to reason effectively, language models could reason more effectively with some intermediate computation that is not in natural language.

content.text:
I now have comprehensive results. Here is the compiled list of relevant academic papers:

---

## Relevant Academic Papers for "An LLM That's Careful With Its Words"

### Foundational Chain-of-Thought Papers

**1. Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models**
- **Authors:** Jason Wei, Xuezhi Wang, Dale Schuurmans, Maarten Bosma, Ed Chi, Quoc Le, Denny Zhou
- **Year:** 2022
- **arXiv:** [2201.11903](https://arxiv.org/abs/2201.11903)
- **Relevance:** The foundational paper. Demonstrates that providing a few chain-of-thought exemplars in prompts enables LLMs to perform multi-step reasoning. A 540B model with 8 CoT exemplars achieved SOTA on GSM8K. Establishes that intermediate reasoning tokens improve output quality.

**2. Large Language Models are Zero-Shot Reasoners ("Let's think step by step")**
- **Authors:** Takeshi Kojima, Shixiang Shane Gu, Machel Reid, Yutaka Matsuo, Yusuke Iwasawa
- **Year:** 2022
- **arXiv:** [2205.11916](https://arxiv.org/abs/2205.11916)
- **Relevance:** Shows that simply prepending "Let's think step by step" triggers multi-hop reasoning across diverse tasks without any few-shot examples. Demonstrates that even a minimal prompt for intermediate computation dramatically improves output quality.

**3. Tree of Thoughts: Deliberate Problem Solving with Large Language Models**
- **Authors:** Shunyu Yao, Dian Yu, Jeffrey Zhao, Izhak Shafran, Thomas L. Griffiths, Yuan Cao, Karthik Narasimhan
- **Year:** 2023
- **arXiv:** [2305.10601](https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.10601)
- **Relevance:** Generalizes CoT by allowing exploration of multiple reasoning paths with self-evaluation and backtracking. Demonstrates deliberate, careful reasoning. GPT-4 went from 4% to 74% success on Game of 24 with this approach.

**4. STaR: Bootstrapping Reasoning With Reasoning**
- **Authors:** Eric Zelikman, Yuhuai Wu, Jesse Mu, Noah D. Goodman
- **Year:** 2022
- **arXiv:** [2203.14465](https://arxiv.org/abs/2203.14465)
- **Relevance:** Teaches LLMs to self-generate rationales through iterative bootstrapping. Models learn to produce step-by-step reasoning that improves their own performance -- a precursor to the idea of models learning to "think carefully."

### Thinking/Pause/Filler Tokens

**5. Think before you speak: Training Language Models With Pause Tokens**
- **Authors:** Sachin Goyal, Ziwei Ji, Ankit Singh Rawat, Aditya Krishna Menon, Sanjiv Kumar, Vaishnavh Nagarajan
- **Year:** 2023 (ICLR 2024)
- **arXiv:** [2310.02226](https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.02226)
- **Relevance:** Directly relevant. Proposes appending learnable "pause" tokens to input, giving the model extra hidden-state computation before generating output. A 1B model showed gains on 8/9 tasks, including 18% improvement on SQuAD. This is the closest prior work to inserting thinking tokens between sentences.

**6. Let's Think Dot by Dot: Hidden Computation in Transformer Language Models**
- **Authors:** Jacob Pfau, William Merrill, Samuel R. Bowman
- **Year:** 2024
- **arXiv:** [2404.15758](https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.15758)
- **Relevance:** Core paper for this research. Shows transformers can use meaningless filler tokens (e.g., "......") to solve hard algorithmic tasks they otherwise cannot. Provides theoretical characterization of when filler tokens help (quantifier depth of first-order formulas). Raises concerns about hidden, unauditable computation.

**7. Pause Tokens Strictly Increase the Expressivity of Constant-Depth Transformers**
- **Authors:** Charles London, Varun Kanade
- **Year:** 2025 (NeurIPS 2025)
- **arXiv:** [2505.21024](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.21024)
- **Relevance:** Provides formal theoretical proof that pause tokens strictly increase transformer expressivity. Without pause tokens, constant-depth transformers compute only a subset of AC0; with them, they can express the entire class. For log-precision transformers, pause tokens achieve TC0 expressivity.

**8. Learning to Insert [PAUSE] Tokens for Better Reasoning**
- **Authors:** Eunki Kim, Sangryul Kim, et al.
- **Year:** 2025
- **arXiv:** [2506.03616](https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.03616)
- **Relevance:** Proposes Dynamic Inserting Tokens Training (DIT), which identifies low-confidence positions in sequences and strategically inserts [PAUSE] tokens there. Shows gains of up to 4.7% on GSM8K. Demonstrates that strategic placement of thinking tokens matters.

### Inner Monologue and Scratchpad Approaches

**9. Show Your Work: Scratchpads for Intermediate Computation with Language Models**
- **Authors:** Maxwell Nye, Anders Johan Andreassen, Guy Gur-Ari, Henryk Michalewski, Jacob Austin, David Biber, David Dohan, Aitor Lewkowycz, Maarten Bosma, David Luan, Charles Sutton, Augustus Odena
- **Year:** 2021
- **arXiv:** [2112.00114](https://arxiv.org/abs/2112.00114)
- **Relevance:** Early demonstration that training LMs to emit intermediate computation steps into a "scratchpad" dramatically improves multi-step computation (long addition, program execution). Foundational work showing intermediate tokens enable computation beyond the model's native depth.

**10. Learning to Reason and Memorize with Self-Notes**
- **Authors:** Jack Lanchantin, Shubham Toshniwal, Jason Weston, Arthur Szlam, Sainbayar Sukhbaatar
- **Year:** 2023 (NeurIPS 2023)
- **arXiv:** [2305.00833](https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.00833)
- **Relevance:** Highly relevant. The model can interleave generated "self-note" tokens with input context, performing reasoning on-the-fly as it reads. These self-notes act as both intermediate reasoning steps and working memory. This is very close to the idea of thinking tokens between every sentence.

**11. Quiet-STaR: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Think Before Speaking**
- **Authors:** Eric Zelikman, Georges Harik, Yijia Shao, Varuna Jayasiri, Nick Haber, Noah D. Goodman
- **Year:** 2024
- **arXiv:** [2403.09629](https://arxiv.org/abs/2403.09629)
- **Relevance:** Most directly relevant paper. LMs learn to generate internal rationales at *every token position* to explain future text. Uses learnable start/end-of-thought tokens. Shows zero-shot improvements on GSM8K (5.9% to 10.9%) and CommonsenseQA (36.3% to 47.2%) without task-specific fine-tuning. This is essentially "thinking tokens between every token."

### Implicit/Latent Reasoning

**12. Implicit Chain of Thought Reasoning via Knowledge Distillation**
- **Authors:** Yuntian Deng, Kiran Prasad, Roland Fernandez, Paul Smolensky, Vishrav Chaudhary, Stuart Shieber
- **Year:** 2023
- **arXiv:** [2311.01460](https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.01460)

Continuous thoughts can encode multiple reasoning paths (BFS-like). Outperforms CoT on logical reasoning tasks with fewer tokens.

**15. Compressed Chain of Thought: Efficient Reasoning Through Dense Representations**
- **Authors:** Jeffrey Cheng, Benjamin Van Durme

- **Relevance:** Introduces "contemplation tokens" -- compressed dense representations of full reasoning chains. These tokens provide extra computation in a compact form, directly relevant to the idea of efficient thinking tokens that don't waste generation budget on verbose text.

### Adaptive Computation

- **Authors:** Alex Graves
- **Year:** 2016
- **arXiv:** [1603.08983](https://arxiv.org/abs/1603.08983)

content.text:
I now have comprehensive results. Here is the compiled list of relevant academic papers:

---

## Relevant Academic Papers for "An LLM That's Careful With Its Words"

### Foundational Chain-of-Thought Papers

**1. Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models**
- **Authors:** Jason Wei, Xuezhi Wang, Dale Schuurmans, Maarten Bosma, Ed Chi, Quoc Le, Denny Zhou
- **Year:** 2022
- **arXiv:** [2201.11903](https://arxiv.org/abs/2201.11903)
- **Relevance:** The foundational paper. Demonstrates that providing a few chain-of-thought exemplars in prompts enables LLMs to perform multi-step reasoning. A 540B model with 8 CoT exemplars achieved SOTA on GSM8K. Establishes that intermediate reasoning tokens improve output quality.

**2. Large Language Models are Zero-Shot Reasoners ("Let's think step by step")**
- **Authors:** Takeshi Kojima, Shixiang Shane Gu, Machel Reid, Yutaka Matsuo, Yusuke Iwasawa
- **Year:** 2022
- **arXiv:** [2205.11916](https://arxiv.org/abs/2205.11916)
- **Relevance:** Shows that simply prepending "Let's think step by step" triggers multi-hop reasoning across diverse tasks without any few-shot examples. Demonstrates that even a minimal prompt for intermediate computation dramatically improves output quality.

**3. Tree of Thoughts: Deliberate Problem Solving with Large Language Models**
- **Authors:** Shunyu Yao, Dian Yu, Jeffrey Zhao, Izhak Shafran, Thomas L. Griffiths, Yuan Cao, Karthik Narasimhan
- **Year:** 2023
- **arXiv:** [2305.10601](https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.10601)
- **Relevance:** Generalizes CoT by allowing exploration of multiple reasoning paths with self-evaluation and backtracking. Demonstrates deliberate, careful reasoning. GPT-4 went from 4% to 74% success on Game of 24 with this approach.

**4. STaR: Bootstrapping Reasoning With Reasoning**
- **Authors:** Eric Zelikman, Yuhuai Wu, Jesse Mu, Noah D. Goodman
- **Year:** 2022
- **arXiv:** [2203.14465](https://arxiv.org/abs/2203.14465)
- **Relevance:** Teaches LLMs to self-generate rationales through iterative bootstrapping. Models learn to produce step-by-step reasoning that improves their own performance -- a precursor to the idea of models learning to "think carefully."

### Thinking/Pause/Filler Tokens

**5. Think before you speak: Training Language Models With Pause Tokens**
- **Authors:** Sachin Goyal, Ziwei Ji, Ankit Singh Rawat, Aditya Krishna Menon, Sanjiv Kumar, Vaishnavh Nagarajan
- **Year:** 2023 (ICLR 2024)
- **arXiv:** [2310.02226](https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.02226)
- **Relevance:** Directly relevant. Proposes appending learnable "pause" tokens to input, giving the model extra hidden-state computation before generating output. A 1B model showed gains on 8/9 tasks, including 18% improvement on SQuAD. This is the closest prior work to inserting thinking tokens between sentences.

**6. Let's Think Dot by Dot: Hidden Computation in Transformer Language Models**
- **Authors:** Jacob Pfau, William Merrill, Samuel R. Bowman
- **Year:** 2024
- **arXiv:** [2404.15758](https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.15758)
- **Relevance:** Core paper for this research. Shows transformers can use meaningless filler tokens (e.g., "......") to solve hard algorithmic tasks they otherwise cannot. Provides theoretical characterization of when filler tokens help (quantifier depth of first-order formulas). Raises concerns about hidden, unauditable computation.

**7. Pause Tokens Strictly Increase the Expressivity of Constant-Depth Transformers**
- **Authors:** Charles London, Varun Kanade
- **Year:** 2025 (NeurIPS 2025)
- **arXiv:** [2505.21024](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.21024)
- **Relevance:** Provides formal theoretical proof that pause tokens strictly increase transformer expressivity. Without pause tokens, constant-depth transformers compute only a subset of AC0; with them, they can express the entire class. For log-precision transformers, pause tokens achieve TC0 expressivity.

**8. Learning to Insert [PAUSE] Tokens for Better Reasoning**
- **Authors:** Eunki Kim, Sangryul Kim, et al.
- **Year:** 2025
- **arXiv:** [2506.03616](https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.03616)
- **Relevance:** Proposes Dynamic Inserting Tokens Training (DIT), which identifies low-confidence positions in sequences and strategically inserts [PAUSE] tokens there. Shows gains of up to 4.7% on GSM8K. Demonstrates that strategic placement of thinking tokens matters.

### Inner Monologue and Scratchpad Approaches

**9. Show Your Work: Scratchpads for Intermediate Computation with Language Models**
- **Authors:** Maxwell Nye, Anders Johan Andreassen, Guy Gur-Ari, Henryk Michalewski, Jacob Austin, David Biber, David Dohan, Aitor Lewkowycz, Maarten Bosma, David Luan, Charles Sutton, Augustus Odena
- **Year:** 2021
- **arXiv:** [2112.00114](https://arxiv.org/abs/2112.00114)
- **Relevance:** Early demonstration that training LMs to emit intermediate computation steps into a "scratchpad" dramatically improves multi-step computation (long addition, program execution). Foundational work showing intermediate tokens enable computation beyond the model's native depth.

**10. Learning to Reason and Memorize with Self-Notes**
- **Authors:** Jack Lanchantin, Shubham Toshniwal, Jason Weston, Arthur Szlam, Sainbayar Sukhbaatar
- **Year:** 2023 (NeurIPS 2023)
- **arXiv:** [2305.00833](https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.00833)
- **Relevance:** Highly relevant. The model can interleave generated "self-note" tokens with input context, performing reasoning on-the-fly as it reads. These self-notes act as both intermediate reasoning steps and working memory. This is very close to the idea of thinking tokens between every sentence.

**11. Quiet-STaR: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Think Before Speaking**
- **Authors:** Eric Zelikman, Georges Harik, Yijia Shao, Varuna Jayasiri, Nick Haber, Noah D. Goodman
- **Year:** 2024
- **arXiv:** [2403.09629](https://arxiv.org/abs/2403.09629)
- **Relevance:** Most directly relevant paper. LMs learn to generate internal rationales at *every token position* to explain future text. Uses learnable start/end-of-thought tokens. Shows zero-shot improvements on GSM8K (5.9% to 10.9%) and CommonsenseQA (36.3% to 47.2%) without task-specific fine-tuning. This is essentially "thinking tokens between every token."

### Implicit/Latent Reasoning

**12. Implicit Chain of Thought Reasoning via Knowledge Distillation**
- **Authors:** Yuntian Deng, Kiran Prasad, Roland Fernandez, Paul Smolensky, Vishrav Chaudhary, Stuart Shieber
- **Year:** 2023
- **arXiv:** [2311.01460](https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.01460)
- **Relevance:** Proposes "vertical" reasoning in hidden states rather than "horizontal" reasoning via token generation. Distills explicit CoT reasoning into implicit hidden-state computation. Shows that reasoning can happen without generating intermediate text tokens.

**13. From Explicit CoT to Implicit CoT: Learning to Internalize CoT Step by Step**
- **Authors:** (Stepwise Internalization)
- **Year:** 2024
- **arXiv:** [2405.14838](https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.14838)
- **Relevance:** Gradually removes intermediate CoT tokens while finetuning the model to internalize reasoning steps. GPT-2 Small achieves high accuracy on 9x9 multiplication; outperforms GPT-4 on GSM8K without outputting any intermediate steps.

**14. Training Large Language Models to Reason in a Continuous Latent Space (Coconut)**
- **Authors:** Shibo Hao, Sainbayar Sukhbaatar, DiJia Su, Xian Li, Zhiting Hu, Jason Weston, Yuandong Tian
- **Year:** 2024
- **arXiv:** [2412.06769](https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.06769)
- **Relevance:** Proposes "Chain of Continuous Thought" where the model's last hidden state is fed back as the next input embedding directly in continuous space, bypassing discrete token generation. Continuous thoughts can encode multiple reasoning paths (BFS-like). Outperforms CoT on logical reasoning tasks with fewer tokens.

**15. Compressed Chain of Thought: Efficient Reasoning Through Dense Representations**
- **Authors:** Jeffrey Cheng, Benjamin Van Durme
- **Year:** 2024
- **arXiv:** [2412.13171](https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.13171)
- **Relevance:** Introduces "contemplation tokens" -- compressed dense representations of full reasoning chains. These tokens provide extra computation in a compact form, directly relevant to the idea of efficient thinking tokens that don't waste generation budget on verbose text.

### Adaptive Computation

**16. Adaptive Computation Time for Recurrent Neural Networks**
- **Authors:** Alex Graves
- **Year:** 2016
- **arXiv:** [1603.08983](https://arxiv.org/abs/1603.08983)
- **Relevance:** Foundational work on allowing neural networks to learn how many computational steps to take per input. The conceptual ancestor of pause/thinking tokens -- the idea that different inputs require different amounts of computation.

---

### Summary of Relevance to Your Research

The most directly relevant papers for "thinking tokens between every sentence" are:

1. **Quiet-STaR** (2403.09629) -- thinking at every token position
2. **Think before you speak / Pause Tokens** (2310.02226) -- extra computation via pause tokens
3. **Self-Notes** (2305.00833) -- interleaving thinking tokens with input
4. **Let's Think Dot by Dot** (2404.15758) -- filler tokens enable hidden computation
5. **Pause Tokens Strictly Increase Expressivity** (2505.21024) -- theoretical proof that extra tokens increase computational power
6. **Learning to Insert [PAUSE] Tokens** (2506.03616) -- strategic placement of pause tokens
7. **Coconut** (2412.06769) -- continuous latent reasoning as an alternative to discrete thinking tokens

The theoretical grounding comes from papers 6, 7, and 16 (expressivity results, adaptive computation). The empirical evidence for careful generation comes from papers 1-4 (CoT prompting family) and 5, 8-11 (pause/scratchpad/self-notes).

"authors": "Daniele Paliotta, Junxiong Wang, Matteo Pagliardini, Kevin Y. Li, Aviv Bick, J. Kolter, Albert Gu, Franccois Fleuret, Tri Dao",
      "url": "https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/9ba7158d20f54a24c24dea101bd9607407eb9ad4",
      "relevance": 1,
      "abstract": "Recent advancements have demonstrated that the performance of large language models (LLMs) can be significantly enhanced by scaling computational resources at test time. A common strategy involves generating multiple Chain-of-Thought (CoT) trajectories and aggregating their outputs through various selection mechanisms. This raises a fundamental question: can models with lower complexity leverage their superior generation throughput to outperform similarly sized Transformers for a fixed computational budget? To address this question and overcome the lack of strong subquadratic reasoners, we distill pure and hybrid Mamba models from pretrained Transformers. Trained on only 8 billion tokens, our distilled models show strong performance and scaling on mathematical reasoning datasets while being much faster at inference for large batches and long sequences. Despite the zero-shot performance hit due to distillation, both pure and hybrid Mamba models can scale their coverage and accuracy performance past their Transformer teacher models under fixed time budgets, opening a new direction for scaling inference compute.",
      "citations": 21
    },
    {
      "title": "Bridging Code Semantic and LLMs: Semantic Chain-of-Thought Prompting for Code Generation",
      "year": 2023,
      "authors": "Yingwei Ma, Yue Yu, Shanshan Li, Yu Jiang, Yong Guo, Yuanliang Zhang, Yutao Xie, Xiangke Liao",
      "url": "https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/b87abda31a8ece913c7b98ee6bff5c0026e51bee",
      "relevance": 1,
      "abstract": "Large language models (LLMs) have showcased remarkable prowess in code generation. However, automated code generation is still challenging since it requires a high-level semantic mapping between natural language requirements and codes. Most existing LLMs-based approaches for code generation rely on decoder-only causal language models often treate codes merely as plain text tokens, i.e., feeding the requirements as a prompt input, and outputing code as flat sequence of tokens, potentially missing the rich semantic features inherent in source code. To bridge this gap, this paper proposes the\"Semantic Chain-of-Thought\"approach to intruduce semantic information of code, named SeCoT. Our motivation is that the semantic information of the source code (\\eg data flow and control flow) describes more precise program execution behavior, intention and function. By guiding LLM consider and integrate semantic information, we can achieve a more granular understanding and representation of code, enhancing code generation accuracy. Meanwhile, while traditional techniques leveraging such semantic information require complex static or dynamic code analysis to obtain features such as data flow and control flow, SeCoT demonstrates that this process can be fully automated via the intrinsic capabilities of LLMs (i.e., in-context learning), while being generalizable and applicable to challenging domains. While SeCoT can be applied with different LLMs, this paper focuses on the powerful GPT-style models: ChatGPT(close-source model) and WizardCoder(open-source model). The experimental study on three popular DL benchmarks (i.e., HumanEval, HumanEval-ET and MBPP) shows that SeCoT can achieves state-of-the-art performance, greatly improving the potential for large models and code generation.",
      "citations": 13
    },
    {
      "title": "MyGO Multiplex CoT: A Method for Self-Reflection in Large Language Models via Double Chain of Thought Thinking",
      "year": 2025,
      "authors": "Shihao Ji, Zihui Song, Fucheng Zhong, Jisen Jia, Zhaobo Wu, Zheyi Cao, Tianhao Xu",
      "url": "https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/92d6b2cc9e60c3d59398a900886cef90646b1c9d",
      "relevance": 1,
      "abstract": "Recent advancements in large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated their impressive abilities in various reasoning and decision-making tasks. However, the quality and coherence of the reasoning process can still benefit from enhanced introspection and self-reflection. In this paper, we introduce Multiplex CoT (Chain of Thought), a method that enables LLMs to simulate a form of self-review while reasoning, by initiating double Chain of Thought (CoT) thinking. Multiplex CoT leverages the power of iterative reasoning, where the model generates an initial chain of thought and subsequently critiques and refines this reasoning with a second round of thought generation. This recursive approach allows for more coherent, logical, and robust answers, improving the overall decision-making process. We demonstrate how this method can be effectively implemented using simple prompt engineering in existing LLM architectures, achieving an effect similar to that of the Learning-Refinement Model (LRM) without the need for additional training. Additionally, we present a practical guide for implementing the method in Google Colab, enabling easy integration into real-world applications.",
      "citations": 4
    },
    {
      "title": "Chain-of-Thought vs. Few-Shot: A Comparative Study of Prompting Strategies for Code Generation",
      "year": 2026,
      "authors": "Koorosh Nobakhtfar, Kenan \u00c7ak\u0131lc\u0131, Ruken Zilan",
      "url": "https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/6afcc4217167db4fe41affa3d59197af5493ca52",
      "relevance": 1,
      "abstract": "The study presented here is an empirical investigation into the performance and cost effects of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting versus contextual Zero-Shot/Few-Shot prompting on GPT-5 across a variety of software engineering tasks. We used a 2x2 factorial design with 80 model-generated functions, measuring accuracy, prompt verbosity, response verbosity, and total API cost. The main finding is that there is a trade-off between better reasoning and higher resource consumption. The two-way ANOVA results indicate that CoT indeed significantly improved accuracy for both evaluators. This confirms the assumption that spelling out intermediate steps decreases black-box errors and improves logical justification. However, CoT also led to a drastic increase in response verbosity (means: 305.20 vs. 166.55 tokens) and the corresponding API costs. On the other hand, the Few-Shot context factor was primarily responsible for input complexity: it greatly increased prompt verbosity (means: 219.55 vs. 50.75 tokens), while leaving response verbosity and cost essentially unchanged. While one assessment indicated that Few-Shot improved accuracy, the other did not, suggesting that Few-Shot examples are effective only in some cases and can sometimes cause confusion rather than improvement. We conclude that, in this GPT-5-based configuration, CoT is best reserved for scenarios where the need for enhanced reasoning outweighs the associated computational overhead. By quantifying this accuracy vs cost tradeoff, our findings guide the selection of cost-efficient prompting strategies. One limitation of this study is that tasks were both generated and responded to by the LLM, thereby limiting its external validity relative to prompts crafted by human engineers.",
      "citations": 0
    },
    {
      "title": "TIBSTC-CoT: A Multi-Domain Instruction Dataset for Chain-of-Thought Reasoning in Language Models",
      "year": 2025,
      "authors": "Fan Gao, Cheng Huang, N. Tashi, Yutong Liu, Xiangxiang Wang, Thupten Tsering, Ma-bao Ban, Renzeg Duojie, Gadeng Luosang, Rinchen Dongrub, Dorje Tashi, Xiao Feng, Hao Wang, Yongbin Yu",
      "url": "https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/02a571f2c38ec25ba1a6ff3c6bf2297cd640bced",
      "relevance": 1,
      "abstract": "To address the severe data scarcity in Tibetan, a low-resource language spoken by over six million people, we introduce TIBSTC-CoT, the large-scale, multi-domain Tibetan dataset automatically constructed via chain-of-thought prompting with large language models (LLMs). TIBSTC-CoT establishes a scalable and reproducible framework for dataset creation in low-resource settings, covering diverse domains and reasoning patterns essential for language understanding and generation. Building on this dataset, we develop the Sunshine-thinking LLM family, a series of Tibetan-centric LLMs equipped with chain-of-thought capabilities. Trained entirely on TIBSTC-CoT, Sunshine-thinking has demonstrated strong reasoning and generation performance, comparable to state-of-the-art (SOTA) multilingual LLMs. Our work marks a significant step toward inclusive AI by enabling high-quality Tibetan language processing through both resource creation and model innovation. All data are available: https://github.com/Vicentvankor/sun-shine.",
      "citations": 2
    },
    {
      "title": "Reasoning as a Resource: Optimizing Fast and Slow Thinking in Code Generation Models",
      "year": 2025,
      "authors": "Zongjie Li, Shuai Wang",
      "url": "https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/4b513c0d04eb246bd6634ab5b75026132da6af10",
      "relevance": 1,
      "abstract": "This position paper proposes a fundamental shift in designing code generation models: treating reasoning depth as a controllable resource. Rather than being an incidental by-product of prompting, we argue that the trade-off between rapid, direct answers (\"fast thinking\") and elaborate, chain-of-thought deliberation (\"slow thinking\") must be explicitly managed. We contend that optimizing reasoning budgets across the entire model lifecycle\u2014from synthetic data creation and benchmarking to real-world deployment\u2014can unlock superior trade-offs among accuracy, latency, and cost. This paper outlines how adaptive control over reasoning can enrich supervision signals, motivate new multi-dimensional benchmarks, and inform cost-aware, security-conscious deployment policies. By viewing fast and slow thinking as complementary modes to be scheduled, we envision coding agents that think deep when necessary and act fast when possible.",
      "citations": 2
    },
    {
      "title": "Intention Chain-of-Thought Prompting with Dynamic Routing for Code Generation",
      "year": 2025,
      "authors": "Shen Li, Li Huang, S. Zhan, Weifeng Sun, Tao Yin, Zhongxin Liu, Meng Yan",
      "url": "https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/2ba73d4a837e873faab218a28a48a3dbada2b135",
      "relevance": 1,
      "abstract": "Large language models (LLMs) exhibit strong generative capabilities and have shown great potential in code generation. Existing chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting methods enhance model reasoning by eliciting intermediate steps, but suffer from two major limitations: First, their uniform application tends to induce overthinking on simple tasks. Second, they lack intention abstraction in code generation, such as explicitly modeling core algorithmic design and efficiency, leading models to focus on surface-level structures while neglecting the global problem objective. Inspired by the cognitive economy principle of engaging structured reasoning only when necessary to conserve cognitive resources, we propose RoutingGen, a novel difficulty-aware routing framework that dynamically adapts prompting strategies for code generation. For simple tasks, it adopts few-shot prompting; for more complex ones, it invokes a structured reasoning strategy, termed Intention Chain-of-Thought (ICoT), which we introduce to guide the model in capturing task intention, such as the core algorithmic logic and its time complexity. Experiments across three models and six standard code generation benchmarks show that RoutingGen achieves state-of-the-art performance in most settings, while reducing total token usage by 46.37% on average across settings. Furthermore, ICoT outperforms six existing prompting baselines on challenging benchmarks.",
      "citations": 0
    },
    {
      "title": "Expert-Guided Prompting and Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Emergency Medical Service Question Answering",
      "year": 2025,
      "authors": "Xueren Ge, Sahil Murtaza, Anthony Cortez, H. Alemzadeh",
      "url": "https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/90d7f9744b13d29c4aa52610aed7999954129b6d",
      "relevance": 1,
      "abstract": "Large language models (LLMs) have shown promise in medical question answering, yet they often overlook the domain-specific expertise that professionals depend on-such as the clinical subject areas (e.g., trauma, airway) and the certification level (e.g., EMT, Paramedic). Existing approaches typically apply general-purpose prompting or retrieval strategies without leveraging this structured context, limiting performance in high-stakes settings. We address this gap with EMSQA, an 24.3K-question multiple-choice dataset spanning 10 clinical subject areas and 4 certification levels, accompanied by curated, subject area-aligned knowledge bases (40K documents and 2M tokens). Building on EMSQA, we introduce (i) Expert-CoT, a prompting strategy that conditions chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning on specific clinical subject area and certification level, and (ii) ExpertRAG, a retrieval-augmented generation pipeline that grounds responses in subject area-aligned documents and real-world patient data. Experiments on 4 LLMs show that Expert-CoT improves up to 2.05% over vanilla CoT prompting. Additionally, combining Expert-CoT with ExpertRAG yields up to a 4.59% accuracy gain over standard RAG baselines. Notably, the 32B expertise-augmented LLMs pass all the computer-adaptive EMS certification simulation exams.",
      "citations": 0
    },
    {
      "title": "Self-Refine: Iterative Refinement with Self-Feedback",
      "year": 2023,
      "authors": "Aman Madaan, Niket Tandon, Prakhar Gupta, Skyler Hallinan, Luyu Gao, Sarah Wiegreffe, Uri Alon, Nouha Dziri, Shrimai Prabhumoye, Yiming Yang, S. Welleck, Bodhisattwa Prasad Majumder, Shashank Gupta, A. Yazdanbakhsh, Peter Clark",
      "url": "https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/3aaf6a2cbad5850ad81ab5c163599cb3d523436f",
      "relevance": 1,
      "abstract": "Like humans, large language models (LLMs) do not always generate the best output on their first try. Motivated by how humans refine their written text, we introduce Self-Refine, an approach for improving initial outputs from LLMs through iterative feedback and refinement. The main idea is to generate an initial output using an LLMs; then, the same LLMs provides feedback for its output and uses it to refine itself, iteratively. Self-Refine does not require any supervised training data, additional training, or reinforcement learning, and instead uses a single LLM as the generator, refiner, and feedback provider. We evaluate Self-Refine across 7 diverse tasks, ranging from dialog response generation to mathematical reasoning, using state-of-the-art (GPT-3.5, ChatGPT, and GPT-4) LLMs. Across all evaluated tasks, outputs generated with Self-Refine are preferred by humans and automatic metrics over those generated with the same LLM using conventional one-step generation, improving by ~20% absolute on average in task performance. Our work demonstrates that even state-of-the-art LLMs like GPT-4 can be further improved at test time using our simple, standalone approach.",
      "citations": 3007
    },
    {
      "title": "Branch-Solve-Merge Improves Large Language Model Evaluation and Generation",
      "year": 2023,
      "authors": "Swarnadeep Saha, Omer Levy, Asli Celikyilmaz, Mohit Bansal, Jason Weston, Xian Li",
      "url": "https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/2218ab2653ed2ab651a03d98056e2d6ae1d99132",
      "relevance": 1,
      "abstract": "Large Language Models (LLMs) are frequently used for multi-faceted language generation and evaluation tasks that involve satisfying intricate user constraints or taking into account multiple aspects and criteria. However, their performance can fall short, due to the model\u2019s lack of coherence and inability to plan and decompose the problem. We propose Branch-Solve-Merge (BSM), a Large Language Model program (Schlag et al., 2023) for tackling such challenging natural language tasks. It consists of branch, solve, and merge modules that are parameterized with specific prompts to the base LLM. These three modules plan a decomposition of the task into multiple parallel sub-tasks, independently solve them, and fuse the solutions to the sub-tasks. We apply our method to the tasks of LLM response evaluation and constrained text generation and evaluate its effectiveness with multiple LLMs, including Vicuna, LLaMA-2-chat, and GPT-4. BSM improves the evaluation correctness and consistency for each LLM by enhancing human-LLM agreement by up to 26%, reducing length and pairwise position biases by up to 50%, and allowing LLaMA-2-chat to match or outperform GPT-4 on most domains. On a constraint story generation task, BSM improves the coherence of stories while also improving constraint satisfaction by 12%.",
      "citations": 110
    },
    {
      "title": "InftyThink: Breaking the Length Limits of Long-Context Reasoning in Large Language Models",
      "year": 2025,
      "authors": "Yuchen Yan, Yongliang Shen, Yang Liu, Jin Jiang, Mengdi Zhang, Jian Shao, Yueting Zhuang",
      "url": "https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/e2e13191a89f5529249e4ecb82493718980a5bc3",
      "relevance": 1,
      "abstract": "Advanced reasoning in large language models has achieved remarkable performance on challenging tasks, but the prevailing long-context reasoning paradigm faces critical limitations: quadratic computational scaling with sequence length, reasoning constrained by maximum context boundaries, and performance degradation beyond pre-training context windows. Existing approaches primarily compress reasoning chains without addressing the fundamental scaling problem. To overcome these challenges, we introduce InftyThink, a paradigm that transforms monolithic reasoning into an iterative process with intermediate summarization. By interleaving short reasoning segments with concise progress summaries, our approach enables unbounded reasoning depth while maintaining bounded computational costs. This creates a characteristic sawtooth memory pattern that significantly reduces computational complexity compared to traditional approaches. Furthermore, we develop a methodology for reconstructing long-context reasoning datasets into our iterative format, transforming OpenR1-Math into 333K training instances. Experiments across multiple model architectures demonstrate that our approach reduces computational costs while improving performance, with Qwen2.5-Math-7B showing 3-11% improvements across MATH500, AIME24, and GPQA_diamond benchmarks. Our work challenges the assumed trade-off between reasoning depth and computational efficiency, providing a more scalable approach to complex reasoning without architectural modifications.",
      "citations": 31
    }
  ]
}
